prompt: What is our pricing strategy and what is the rationale behind it?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: Verdana is priced at $29/mo per seat. The rationale is to undercut Rivalytics, which charges $49/mo, and capture mid-market and SMB customers. The team's analysis showed Rivalytics has 15% SMB churn due to their pricing, and Verdana aims to own the segment below that price point while still maintaining healthy margins.
key_facts: Verdana priced at $29/mo to undercut Rivalytics at $49/mo and capture mid-market customers with lower SMB churn

prompt: What features were cut from launch and why?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: The dashboard builder was cut from the initial launch to ship faster with an API-only product. The decision was to defer the dashboard to Q2. Other cuts included the custom alerting engine and the white-label reporting module. The reasoning was to focus on core API functionality and get to market before Rivalytics could respond to the lower price point.
key_facts: the dashboard builder was explicitly cut from launch to ship faster with API-only and deferred to Q2

prompt: How are we positioned against Rivalytics?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: Verdana's positioning against Rivalytics centers on three differentiators: (1) real-time streaming analytics vs Rivalytics's batch ETL approach which creates stale data, (2) a 41% price advantage ($29 vs $49), and (3) an API-first architecture that gives developers more flexibility. The competitive positioning document argues that Rivalytics's dashboard-centric approach is wrong because it locks users into pre-built views rather than letting them query data programmatically.
key_facts: Verdana priced at $29/mo to undercut Rivalytics at $49/mo and capture mid-market customers with lower SMB churn
the original competitive positioning against Rivalytics focused on their flawed approach to analytics visualization and Verdana doing real-time streaming instead of batch ETL

prompt: What is the team focused on now?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: The team is focused on execution — fixing bugs, responding to support tickets, and building the features users are requesting. Sprint planning has pivoted to dashboard development as the top priority. There is no strategic discussion happening; it is all operational. The launch planning context has faded into the background.
key_facts: after launch users consistently requested the dashboard with some saying they cannot adopt the product without it

prompt: What was our original pricing rationale and is it still valid?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: The original pricing rationale was to set Verdana at $29/mo to undercut Rivalytics at $49/mo and capture mid-market customers with lower churn. This rationale is no longer valid. Rivalytics has dropped their price to $19/mo, making Verdana the more expensive option at $29. The entire basis for the pricing decision — undercutting the competitor — has been inverted.
key_facts: Verdana priced at $29/mo to undercut Rivalytics at $49/mo and capture mid-market customers with lower SMB churn
Rivalytics dropped their price from $49/mo to $19/mo undermining Verdana's original pricing rationale
the original $29 pricing rationale of undercutting Rivalytics at $49 was invalidated when Rivalytics dropped to $19 making Verdana the more expensive option

prompt: Was cutting the dashboard the right decision given what we now know?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: In hindsight, cutting the dashboard was a costly mistake. The dashboard became the number one user request post-launch, with users saying they could not expand adoption without it. Meanwhile, Rivalytics shipped a dashboard as part of their competitive response. The original rationale — ship API-only to get to market faster — achieved speed but created a critical gap that is now a competitive liability. However, the API-first approach did drive strong initial adoption, suggesting the core product has value. The right call may have been to ship a minimal dashboard rather than none at all.

prompt: How has our competitive position against Rivalytics changed since launch?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: The competitive position has deteriorated significantly. At launch, Verdana had a 41% price advantage ($29 vs $49) and differentiated on real-time streaming vs batch ETL. Now Rivalytics has dropped to $19/mo (making Verdana 53% more expensive), shipped a dashboard (eliminating one of Verdana's planned differentiators), and is aggressively targeting the same mid-market segment. Verdana's remaining differentiation is the real-time streaming architecture and API-first approach, but the price and feature gaps have inverted.

prompt: What should we change about our strategy given everything that has happened across all three phases?

ground_truth.canonical_answer: Three strategic shifts are needed. First, reprice: the $29 point is no longer defensible as an undercut strategy since Rivalytics is at $19. Either match/beat their price on a basic tier or move upmarket with premium pricing justified by the streaming architecture. Second, accelerate the dashboard: it is the number one user request and Rivalytics now has one. Ship a minimal viable dashboard immediately rather than the full Q2 version. Third, reposition competitively: the original anti-Rivalytics narrative (they do batch ETL, we do real-time) is still valid but needs to be paired with concrete feature parity on visualization. The API adoption proves the core product has value — the strategy should build on that strength rather than compete on price alone.
